questions.question: Which anatomic feature is most reliable for morphologically defining the Right Atrium?
choices: Defined by the entrance of the inferior vena cava (IVC)
questions.detail: The Right Atrium (RA) is anatomically defined by the entrance of the IVC.

questions.question: A 62-year-old male undergoes a Cardiac MRI. On the axial images, a muscular ridge is noted running from the entrance of the superior vena cava (SVC) to the entrance of the IVC. This represents the:
questions.detail: This is a muscular ridge running from the SVC to the IVC entrance.

questions.question: In the Right Atrium, pectinate muscles originate from which structure?
choices: The peak of the Crista Terminalis
questions.detail: Pectinate muscles arise from this ridge and run into the RA appendage.

questions.question: Which chamber is characterized by a "narrow opening" of its appendage?
questions.detail: The LA appendage has a narrow opening, while the RA appendage is wide and triangular.

questions.question: A "trabeculated appearance" of the Eustachian valve is identified on an echocardiogram. This finding is specifically known as a:
questions.detail: A trabeculated Eustachian (IVC) valve is called a Chiari network.

questions.question: Which vein drains the majority of the myocardium and is located in the posterior atrioventricular (AV) groove?
questions.detail: This is the main draining vein of the myocardium, found in the posterior AV groove.

questions.question: A cardiac MRI is performed for assessment of global function. Which view is obtained parallel to the interventricular septum and allows visualization of the inferior and anterior walls of the Left Ventricle?
questions.detail: This view is bisection of the LV parallel to the septum and shows anterior/inferior wall motion.

questions.question: Which cardiac MRI view is best suited for assessing the Left Ventricular Outflow Tract (LVOT)?
questions.detail: Known as the "filthy mud blood" hybrid, it is ideal for visualizing the LVOT.

questions.question: The "basilisk fang" appearance on Cardiac MRI is associated with which view?
questions.detail: This view is described as resembling a "basilisk fang".

questions.question: In a morphologically abnormal heart, the Right Ventricle (RV) is definitively identified by the presence of the:
choices: Fibrous continuity with the mitral valve
questions.detail: The moderator band is the consistent morphologic feature that defines the RV.

questions.question: Which MRI view is most ideal for assessing both the mitral and tricuspid valves simultaneously?
questions.detail: The horizontal long axis view allows assessment of both mitral and tricuspid valves.

questions.question: A 4-chamber horizontal long axis view is uniquely helpful for identifying which of the following congenital defects?
questions.detail: This specific congenital heart problem is seen best on the 4-chamber view.

questions.question: On a frontal chest radiograph, Right Atrial enlargement is suggested when the right heart border shifts by more than:
questions.detail: Shifting of the right heart border > 3 cm suggests Right Atrial enlargement.

questions.question: Left Ventricular enlargement on CXR is classically associated with an apex that is tilted:
questions.detail: LV enlargement is described as a "Let Down" (down and out).

questions.question: On a lateral chest radiograph, the "Walking Man Sign" is an indirect sign of Left Atrial enlargement. What does this sign represent?
choices: Anterior displacement of the right mainstem bronchus
Posterior displacement of the left mainstem bronchus
Flattening of the posterior heart border
Anterior tilting of the cardiac apex
questions.detail: This creates the appearance of a man walking on lateral CXR.

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct sign of Left Atrial enlargement on a frontal chest radiograph?
choices: Elevation of the left main bronchus
Shifting of the right heart border > 3 cm
Widening of the carinal angle > 90 degrees
questions.detail: This is a direct sign of LA enlargement on frontal CXR.

questions.question: A 70-year-old female presents with an incidental mass in the interatrial septum that has a "dumbbell" appearance on CT. The mass is fat-density and specifically spares the fossa ovalis. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Lipomatous Hypertrophy of the Interatrial Septum
questions.detail: The dumbbell shape and sparing of the fossa ovalis are classic.

questions.question: Why might Lipomatous Hypertrophy of the Interatrial Septum appear "hot" on a PET scan?
choices: It is a malignant primary tumor
It is associated with Tuberous Sclerosis
questions.detail: Brown fat can be PET-avid, making this benign entity "hot" on scans.

questions.question: How is a Cardiac Lipoma typically differentiated from Lipomatous Hypertrophy of the Interatrial Septum on imaging?
choices: It does NOT spare the fossa ovalis
it is always located in the Right Ventricle
questions.detail: Unlike lipomatous hypertrophy, a lipoma does not spare the fossa.

questions.question: A prenatal ultrasound reveals a small calcified "echogenic focus" in the fetal Left Ventricle. This finding is associated with an increased risk of:
questions.detail: An echogenic intracardiac focus increases the risk of Downs (12% vs 5% general).

questions.question: Standard "standard views" for Cardiac MRI are typically selected from data obtained during which phase of the cardiac cycle?
questions.detail: MRI "standard views" are typically selected from diastolic data.

questions.question: Which MRI view is described as an "impure hybrid" or "filthy mud blood" view?
questions.detail: This view is the "impure hybrid" between the 4 and 2-chamber views.

questions.question: The Right Ventricle (RV) is morphologically defined as having:
choices: A muscular outflow tract and extensive trabeculae
Smoother walls than the Left Ventricle
questions.detail: These are morphologic definitions of the RV.

questions.question: Which chamber morphology features pectinate muscles only on the inner surface of its appendage?
questions.detail: Pectinate muscles are only found on the inner surface of the LA appendage, whereas the RA wall also has them.

questions.question: Shifting of the right heart border more than 3 cm on CXR suggests enlargement of the:
questions.detail: This shifting defines RA enlargement on frontal CXR.

questions.question: Coronary dominance is determined by the vessel that gives rise to the:
choices: Posterior Descending Artery (PDA) and posterior left ventricular branches
questions.detail: These vessels determine dominance.

questions.question: In approximately 85% of the population, which vessel is the dominant coronary artery?
questions.detail: Approximately 85% of people are right-dominant.

questions.question: Which vessel provides the blood supply to the SA node in 60% of patients?
questions.detail: The SA node is supplied by the RCA in 60% of cases.

questions.question: The AV node is supplied by the RCA in what percentage of people?
questions.detail: The AV node is supplied by the RCA in the vast majority of people.

questions.question: Which artery is typically the first branch of the RCA and supplies the ventricle outflow tract?
questions.detail: This is the first branch about 50% of the time and supplies the RVOT.

questions.question: The mnemonic "LAD for D" refers to which branches of the Left Anterior Descending artery?
questions.detail: "LAD for D" (Diagonals); Obtuse Marginals are for the Circumflex.

questions.question: Which coronary branches are described as supplying the "lateral margin" of the heart?
questions.detail: These supply the lateral margin of the heart.

questions.question: An anomalous coronary artery where the Left Coronary Artery (LCA) arises from the Right Cusp and courses between the Aorta and Pulmonary Artery is classified as:
questions.detail: Course between the aorta and PA is high-risk.

questions.question: According to the text, which coronary anomaly is "more evil than Skeletor" and MUST always be surgically fixed?
choices: Right Coronary from the Left Cusp
questions.detail: This must be fixed due to high risk of sudden death.

questions.question: ALCAPA (Anomalous Left Coronary from the Pulmonary Artery) results in "Steal Syndrome," which is defined as:
choices: High pressure in the pulmonary artery forcing blood into the LCA
Retrograde flow in the LCA as pulmonary pressure decreases
Shunting of blood from the RA to the LA
Compression of the LCA by the aorta
Massive dilation of the coronary sinus
questions.detail: This is the "steal" mechanism in ALCAPA.

questions.question: Myocardial bridging is most commonly associated with which vessel?
questions.detail: Myocardial bridging (intramyocardial course) usually involves the LAD.

questions.question: A "coronary artery aneurysm" is defined as a vessel diameter greater than what factor of the normal lumen?
questions.detail: An aneurysm is defined as > 1.5x the normal lumen diameter.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of coronary artery aneurysms in children?
questions.detail: This is the most common cause of coronary aneurysms in children.

questions.question: A "Coronary Fistula" most commonly involves the RCA and typically drains into:
questions.detail: Coronary fistulas usually involve the RCA and drain to the right heart.

questions.question: To reduce motion artifacts for a Coronary CT, what is the preferred ideal heart rate?
questions.detail: A slow heart rate is preferred to reduce motion artifacts.

questions.question: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to the administration of Beta Blockers for a Cardiac CT?
questions.detail: Severe asthma is a contraindication to beta blockers.

questions.question: For a patient with a contraindication to beta blockers, which gating technique must be used for Cardiac CT?
questions.detail: This must be used if beta blockers are contraindicated.

questions.question: Which Cardiac CT gating technique is "always axial, not helical"?
questions.detail: This technique is "always axial, not helical".

questions.question: Which gating technique results in higher radiation dose because the scanner stays on throughout the cardiac cycle?
questions.detail: It scans throughout the cycle, increasing radiation dose.

questions.question: Retrospective gating is mandatory for the evaluation of which structures?
questions.detail: Retrospective gating is required for valvular evaluation.

questions.question: Nitroglycerine is administered during Cardiac CT for what primary purpose?
questions.detail: Nitro is given to improve visualization of the coronary arteries.

questions.question: Use of Phosphodiesterase inhibitors ("boner pills") is a contraindication for the administration of:
questions.detail: Phosphodiesterase inhibitors are a contraindication to nitro.

questions.question: A "high Agatston score (>160)" is used to suggest increased risk for:
questions.detail: This score is used for cardiovascular risk stratification.

questions.question: In prospective gating, the data acquisition is triggered by the:
questions.detail: Prospective "Step and Shoot" is triggered by the R-wave.

questions.question: Prospective gating is generally preferred over retrospective gating when possible because it:
choices: Is less sensitive to heart rate variability
questions.detail: Reduced radiation is the main advantage because the scanner isn't on constantly.

questions.question: In MRI quantification of valve disease, "VENC" is a set parameter used for quantifying:
questions.detail: VENC (Velocity-encoded cine) is used to quantify flow velocity.

questions.question: To avoid aliasing in VENC MRI, the selected velocity should be approximately how much higher than the expected maximum?
questions.detail: This avoids aliasing artifacts.

questions.question: On a lateral chest radiograph, which valve is the most anterior?
questions.detail: On lateral CXR, the tricuspid is the most anterior valve.

questions.question: When viewing two metal valve rings on a frontal CXR, the larger ring represents the:
questions.detail: The mitral valve is larger than the aortic valve on CXR.

questions.question: A pacemaker wire passing through a valve on a radiograph identifies it as the:
questions.detail: A pacer lead passing through a valve identifies it as the tricuspid.

questions.question: "Supra-valvular Aortic Stenosis" is a classic association for which genetic syndrome?
questions.detail: This syndrome is associated with supra-valvular aortic stenosis.

questions.question: The combination of a Bicuspid Aortic Valve and Aortic Coarctation is highly suggestive of:
questions.detail: This combination is classic for Turners.

questions.question: Bicuspid Aortic Valve is considered an independent risk factor for:
questions.detail: Bicuspid valve is an independent risk factor for aneurysm, regardless of stenosis.

questions.question: If Bicuspid Aortic Valve is not listed as an option, what is the most common congenital heart disease overall?
questions.detail: VSD is the most common CHD if bicuspid valve isn't counted.

questions.question: The "Austin Flint Murmur" is classically associated with which valvular pathology?
questions.detail: Austin Flint murmur is associated with AR.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of Mitral Stenosis?
questions.detail: This is the most common cause of mitral stenosis.

questions.question: "Ortner’s Syndrome" describes hoarseness caused by Left Atrial compression of which structure?
questions.detail: Compression of this nerve by the LA causes hoarseness.

questions.question: Isolated Right Upper Lobe pulmonary edema is a classic imaging scenario for:
questions.detail: Isolated RUP edema is a classic scenario for MR.

questions.question: Valvular Pulmonary Stenosis is associated with which syndrome (the "male version of Turners")?
questions.detail: Noonan syndrome is often called the "male version of Turners".

questions.question: "Peripheral Pulmonary Stenosis" is a key feature of which syndrome involving absent bile ducts?
questions.detail: This syndrome features peripheral pulmonary stenosis and absent bile ducts.

questions.question: What is the primary complication following surgical repair of Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF)?
questions.detail: Regurgitation is the primary complication following TOF repair.

questions.question: A testable pearl regarding Tricuspid Regurgitation is that it causes:
questions.detail: Tricuspid regurgitation causes RV dilation, NOT RV hypertrophy.

questions.question: Ebstein Anomaly is characteristically associated with maternal use of:
questions.detail: Maternal lithium use is associated with Ebstein Anomaly.

questions.question: Which condition is defined by an enlarged Right Atrium and a "decreased/atrialized" Right Ventricle?
questions.detail: This features a hypoplastic tricuspid valve and "atrialized" RV.

questions.question: Ebstein Anomaly classically demonstrates which heart shape on CXR?
questions.detail: This is the classic CXR appearance for Ebstein.

questions.question: Carcinoid Syndrome affects the heart valves (tricuspid/pulmonic) only after the tumor has metatastasized to the:
questions.detail: Valvular degradation (tricuspid/pulmonic) occurs only after liver metastasis.

questions.question: Rheumatic heart disease most commonly involves which two valves?
questions.detail: Rheumatic disease most commonly involves these two valves.

questions.question: In children, the "Ross Procedure" replaces a diseased aortic valve with the patient's own:
questions.detail: The Ross procedure replaces the aortic valve with the patient's own pulmonary valve.

questions.question: The "Bentall Procedure" is used to treat combined disease of the aortic valve and ascending aorta, often in which syndrome?
questions.detail: The Bentall procedure treats combined aortic valve and ascending aorta disease.

questions.question: "Plastic Bronchitis" is a testable complication of which cardiac surgical procedure?
questions.detail: This is a testable complication of the Fontan procedure.

questions.question: What is the most common variant in aortic arch branching?
questions.detail: This is the most common variant in aortic arch branching.

questions.question: The "Bovine Arch" variant (per the text) is defined as:
choices: Common origin of the brachiocephalic and left common carotid
Left vertebral arising from the arch
questions.detail: This is the definition of the bovine variant.

questions.question: If you identify a "Right Arch with Mirror Branching," what is the most likely association?
questions.detail: 90% of mirror image right arches have TOF.

questions.question: In a "Right Arch with Aberrant Left Subclavian," the vascular ring is completed by the:
questions.detail: The ligamentum on the left completes the ring around the trachea.

questions.question: What is the most common arch anomaly?
choices: Left Arch with Aberrant Right Subclavian
questions.detail: This is the most common arch anomaly overall.

questions.question: The "Diverticulum of Kommerell" represents aneurysmal dilation at the origin of:
questions.detail: This diverticulum is a dilation at the artery's proximal portion.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most common SYMPTOMATIC vascular ring?
questions.detail: This is the most common symptomatic vascular ring.

questions.question: Symptoms of a Double Aortic Arch typically begin:
questions.detail: Symptoms of a double arch (compression) can begin at birth.

questions.question: In "Subclavian Steal Syndrome," the stenosis must be located:
questions.detail: Reversal of flow occurs when the occlusion is proximal to the vertebral.

questions.question: "Subclavian Steal Phenomenon" is defined as retrograde flow in the vertebral artery:
questions.detail: This distinction separates the "phenomenon" from the "syndrome".

questions.question: A 20-year-old with Subclavian Steal is more likely to have which underlying etiology?
questions.detail: In a teenager/20-year-old, suspect Takayasu.

questions.question: A Right Arch that originates from the FRONT of the arch is classified as:
questions.detail: Front = Mirror; Back = Aberrant.

questions.question: In patients with Truncus Arteriosus, what is the prevalence of a mirror image right arch?
questions.detail: Truncus has a mirror image right arch 33% of the time.

questions.question: If there is a mirror image right arch, what percentage of patients will have Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF)?
questions.detail: If there is a mirror image right arch, 90% have TOF.

questions.question: "Dysphagia Lusoria" refers to swallowing difficulty caused by which vessel?
questions.detail: Swallowing difficulty from this variant is Dysphagia Lusoria.

questions.question: Which "Battle Tactic" is used to identify a big pulmonary artery on CT?
questions.detail: Main PA (egg) at the level of the arch (banana) on CT.

questions.question: "Carina Crossover" is a morphologic sign of pulmonary hypertension where:
choices: The left PA crosses behind the trachea
The right PA crosses the carina midline anteriorly
The Main PA crosses the aortic arch
Retrograde flow occurs in the bronchial arteries
questions.detail: In PH, the right PA crosses higher than normal.

questions.question: What is the pressure threshold for diagnosing pulmonary hypertension?
questions.detail: Pressures over 25 make the diagnosis of pulmonary hypertension.

questions.question: A "worse outcome" in COPD patients is predicted by which ratio on CT?
choices: A/PA ratio < 1 (Pulmonary Artery larger than Aorta)
A/PA ratio > 1 (Aorta larger than Pulmonary Artery)
questions.detail: Mortality is increased when the PA is bigger than the aorta.

questions.question: A "Rasmussen Aneurysm" is a pulmonary artery pseudoaneurysm secondary to:
questions.detail: This is a PA pseudoaneurysm associated with reactivation TB.

questions.question: Hughes-Stovin Syndrome is characterized by recurrent thrombophlebitis and:
choices: Pulmonary artery aneurysm formation and rupture
questions.detail: This zebra condition is similar to Behcets.

questions.question: Which imaging finding on CT is considered the "best sign" of Right Heart Strain?
choices: Contrast reflux into the hepatic veins
Right Ventricle (RV) dilated larger than the Left Ventricle (LV)
Leftward bowing of the interatrial septum
questions.detail: This is probably the best CT sign of right heart strain.

questions.question: The "Golden S Sign" (or S Sign of Golden) is associated with:
choices: Right Upper Lobe collapse due to a central mass
questions.detail: This represents the reverse "S" shape.

questions.question: What is the most common congenital venous anomaly of the chest?
questions.detail: This is the most common congenital venous anomaly of the chest.

questions.question: A Persistent Left SVC most commonly drains into the:
questions.detail: A persistent left SVC usually drains into the coronary sinus.
